title: IV Flow Rate & Dosage Calculation
subtitle: The nurse safely calculates IV drip rates and drug doses — using the formula method, checking the drop factor, verifying a safe dose, and double-checking high-risk math.

patient.name: IV Flow Rate & Dosage
patient.admitDate: The nurse calculates and verifies IV rates and drug doses before giving them.

labs.r: mg vs g, kg vs lb.
Cannot give part of a drop.

nursesNote: The nurse calculates and verifies every IV rate and drug dose before administration. For an infusion pump the rate is programmed in mL/hr (total volume divided by total hours). For gravity tubing the nurse calculates gtt/min using the formula (volume in mL x the drop factor) / time in minutes, and the drop factor is found on the tubing package — macrodrip sets deliver 10-20 gtt/mL and microdrip (mini-drip) sets deliver 60 gtt/mL. Drug doses use the formula Desired dose / dose on Hand x Quantity, and all units must be converted to match (for example mg to g, or pounds to kilograms) before calculating. For weight-based and pediatric drugs the nurse confirms the ordered mg/kg dose falls within the safe reference range. Answers to gtt/min are rounded to whole drops because a partial drop cannot be delivered. The nurse always sanity-checks that the result is reasonable to catch tenfold errors, and performs an independent two-nurse check for high-alert medications such as insulin and heparin and for pediatric doses.
providerOrders: Calculate the pump rate in mL/hr for ordered infusions
Calculate gtt/min using the drop factor for gravity tubing
Verify all units match before calculating a dose
Confirm weight-based doses are within the safe range
Perform an independent two-nurse check for high-alert math

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about IV and dose calculation?

opts.text: Pump rate in mL/hr is total volume divided by total hours
gtt/min uses the tubing's drop factor
The drop factor is printed on the tubing package
Units can be left unmatched when calculating a dose
The dose formula is Desired / Have x Quantity
Sanity-checking the answer catches 10x errors
Microdrip tubing delivers 60 gtt/mL
Weight-based doses should be checked against a safe range
gtt/min is rounded to whole drops
High-alert drug math needs an independent two-nurse check
A pound weight can be used directly as kilograms
gtt/min is only used with gravity tubing, not a pump

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order calculating a gravity IV drip rate

opts.text: Read the order: volume and time
Find the drop factor on the tubing package
Calculate gtt/min with the formula
Round to whole drops and sanity-check
Set the drip and monitor the rate

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A weight-based dose looks 10x too high
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize the dose seems unreasonable
Hold the dose; do not give it
Recheck the math and unit conversions
Clarify with a second nurse and the prescriber
Give the corrected dose and document
Converting pounds to kilograms before calculating
Using the wrong drop factor for the tubing
Sanity-checking that the answer is reasonable
Giving a weight-based dose without checking the range
Rounding gtt/min to whole drops
Skipping the two-nurse check on a heparin drip
Programming mL/hr on the infusion pump
Assuming the answer is right without rechecking
